topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation to Dr. Thorne’s practice felt like a deliberate, calculated insult. I stared at the embossed card, the crisp paper mocking the mess of my memory. Mark. Mark, the boy who used to trip me in the hallway, whose sneer was the soundtrack to my early adolescence, was now a licensed professional. A therapist. My jaw tightened so hard I thought my teeth might crack. I paced my small apartment, the floorboards groaning under my sudden, agitated movements. How could he? The sheer audacity of it made my stomach churn. I remembered the way his eyes used to narrow when he was being cruel, the pure, unadulterated malice. Now, those same eyes were supposed to be filled with gentle understanding. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over his number, a strange mixture of fury and bewildered fascination swirling inside me.

[story 2]

"You really think this is funny?" I snapped, the words tasting metallic on my tongue. I stood in the sterile waiting room, the beige walls amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. Dr. Thorne, with his calm, measured voice and impeccably tailored suit, offered a sympathetic nod. I felt a hot flush creep up my neck, and I gripped my handbag so tightly my knuckles were white. It was impossible. Mark, the architect of my deepest insecurities, was now the one supposed to help fix them. I wanted to scream, to shatter the quiet professionalism of the room. Every perfectly modulated sentence he spoke sounded like a carefully constructed lie designed to soothe the very wounds he’d inflicted. I shifted my weight constantly, an almost violent need to disrupt the serene atmosphere vibrating off him.

[story 3]

I watched him from across the room, a tight knot forming in my chest. He was laughing now, a soft, knowing sound that used to belong to the mocking laughter of my childhood tormentor. It was sickening. I felt a sharp, almost physical recoil, as if a sudden cold draft had hit me. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, my breathing becoming shallow and rapid. This transformation—from petty tormentor to healer—felt fundamentally wrong, a betrayal of everything I thought I knew about him. I wanted to demand an explanation, to force him to acknowledge the damage he’d done, but the words caught in my throat, strangled by a rising tide of pure, simmering resentment. I just stood there, radiating a palpable sense of profound disapproval.
